---
title: "Resolver Strategies & History"
tagline: "Or what to Do When the Same requirements.txt Builds Two Different Environments"
type: scenario
tool: python
---

#### ! Scenario

*Or what to Do When the Same requirements.txt Builds Two Different Environments*
There is no JVM sibling for this one — it is pure Python. The same input can produce a *different* installed environment depending on which resolver, and which resolver *version*, processes it. Here `demo-lib-a` is unpinned but `demo-lib-c` is pinned to `1.0.0`; the newest `demo-lib-a` (2.0.0) needs `demo-lib-c 2.0.0`, so a correct resolver must **backtrack** to `demo-lib-a 1.0.0`.

::: {.callout-note title="Tool Versions"}
Tested with **pip 25.3**, **Poetry 2.4**, **uv 0.11** — all three backtrack correctly. The history matters: **pip < 20.3** used a greedy "first found wins" resolver that did *not* backtrack and would install a broken environment. Backtracking became opt-in at 20.3, default at 21.0, and the only option from 22.2.
:::

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph TD
    subgraph greedy["Greedy (pip < 20.3)"]
        A1[take demo-lib-a 2.0.0] --> B1[needs demo-lib-c 2.0.0]
        B1 --> X1[clash with pin 1.0.0 — installed anyway, broken]
    end
    subgraph backtrack["Backtracking (pip 21+, poetry, uv)"]
        A2[try demo-lib-a 2.0.0] --> B2[needs demo-lib-c 2.0.0: clash]
        B2 --> C2[backtrack: demo-lib-a 1.0.0] --> D2[needs demo-lib-c 1.0.0: OK]
    end
```

```{.text include="pip/requirements.txt" snippet="p3-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
`demo-lib-a` resolves to its newest version, 2.0.0.

#### ! Actual
`demo-lib-a` resolves to **1.0.0**. The modern resolver tries 2.0.0, discovers it forces `demo-lib-c 2.0.0` against your `==1.0.0` pin, abandons that branch, and backs up to `demo-lib-a 1.0.0` — which is compatible. The environment is correct, but *not* the newest-of-everything you might have assumed.

#### ! Why
Dependency resolution is a search, and the resolver's strategy decides the answer. Greedy resolution takes the first candidate and never reconsiders — fast, but it can paint itself into a corner and (pre-20.3) install the corner anyway. Backtracking treats the whole graph as a constraint problem and searches for a version set that satisfies all of it. This is exactly the book's thesis made concrete: **resolution is mechanical, not semantic** — change the mechanism and the same declaration yields a different world.

#### ! Fix
*   **Be on a modern resolver.** pip ≥ 21.0, any Poetry, any uv. If you are stuck on ancient pip, `--use-feature=2020-resolver` (20.3–21) or just upgrade.
*   **Pin what you actually depend on.** Backtracking finds *a* solution; a lock file (see [P5](../scenario-5-locks/guide.qmd)) records *which* one so it's reproducible.

#### ! Risk
* LOW Relying on the modern resolver to find a compatible set.
* MEDIUM Assuming "unpinned" means "newest" — backtracking may legitimately hand you an older version to satisfy a pin elsewhere.
* HIGH Running production installs on pip < 20.3, where an unsatisfiable graph installs silently and breaks at runtime.

#### ! Check

**Fast check:**

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt && pip show demo-lib-a   # Version: 1.0.0
poetry lock && grep -A1 'name = "demo-lib-a"' poetry.lock  # 1.0.0
uv lock && grep -A1 'name = "demo-lib-a"' uv.lock          # 1.0.0
```

**What to look for:** `demo-lib-a` pinned to `1.0.0`, not `2.0.0` — proof the resolver backtracked rather than greedily taking the newest.

**Conclusion:** The resolver chose an older `demo-lib-a` to keep the whole graph satisfiable.

#### ! Takeaway
The same `requirements.txt` is not one environment — it is one environment *per resolver*. Know which resolver you run, and pin the result so the search only has to happen once.

#### ! Related
*   [P2](../scenario-2-conflict/guide.qmd) - When backtracking runs out of options and there is no solution at all.
*   [P5](../scenario-5-locks/guide.qmd) - Freezing the resolved set so it stops depending on the resolver.

#### ! Wild
The 2020 pip resolver change was one of the most consequential — and most disruptive — events in Python packaging. Funded by a grant and years in the making, it broke countless CI pipelines that had been quietly relying on the greedy resolver installing mutually incompatible packages. "It used to work" was true; it had just never been *correct*.
